伦敦(CNN商业报道)英国政府向一家举办性派对的公司投资17万英镑(221,780美元)。
周三，在伦敦和纽约组织女性主导的成人派对的Killing Kitens表示，它已经从英国政府的未来基金(Future Fund)获得了这笔投资，该基金旨在帮助初创企业在冠状病毒大流行中幸存下来。
该公司首席执行官艾玛·塞尔(Emma Sayle)在谈到英国首相鲍里斯·约翰逊(Boris Johnson)时说，我从未想过鲍里斯会是一个睡觉的伴侣。“杀死小猫”已经登上了数字舞台，我们现在是世界上发展最快的成人社交网络的先驱，她补充道。
Sayle于2005年创立了Killing Kitten，据其网站介绍，该公司在独家场所组织仅限会员参加的蒙面派对，重点是追求女性的快乐。它现在在12个国家拥有18万会员，在冠状病毒关闭期间，其网站的流量增长了330%。
这场流行病迫使该公司将所有活动和研讨会都转移到了网上，加快了进军性科技行业的现有计划-这是一个快速增长的行业，包括专注于增强性体验的产品和业务。
英国政府的大部分资金将用于营销和产品创新，包括一款约会应用程序。Sayle说，Killing Kitens希望与Match.com和Bumble等约会应用程序竞争，这两个应用程序在冠状病毒期间的需求也出现了上升，并计划为内衣和性玩具公司推出一个数字广告平台。
性技术学院(Sextech School)的创始人布莱尼·科尔(Bryony Cole)说，性科技是少数几个从2020年起将处于更有利地位的行业之一。该学校向企业家和投资者传授市场知识。特别是约会应用、性玩具和色情在大流行期间蓬勃发展。我们在网上寻找更多的亲密关系或娱乐方式，她告诉CNN商业频道。
CES是世界上最大的科技贸易展之一，其组织者估计，今年1月在拉斯维加斯举行的CES展会上，大约有十几家性科技公司展示了创新产品。
其中一家公司是Dame，一家自称是性健康的公司，该公司周三表示，与一年前相比，其上半年的销售额翻了一番多。去年，该公司在拒绝了Dame&39；的广告后起诉纽约市交通系统，登上了新闻头条。
Killing Kitens表示，根据前几轮融资，该公司的估值为1,150万加元(合1,490万美元)，最近在众筹平台Seedrs上筹集了17万加元(合221,780美元)。这些收益得到了未来基金的匹配，该基金向公司提供125,000英镑(162,000美元)至500万GB(650万美元)的贷款，条件是至少要从私人投资者那里获得匹配的资金。
根据英国财政部的数据，自5月份推出该基金以来，已有800多家企业获得了该基金，批准了近5.13亿GB(6.649亿美元)的贷款。据该公司网站介绍，素食餐厅Stand&amp；Glory是另一个获奖者。
note_en:
London (CNN Business)The British government is investing £170,000 ($221,780) in a company that throws sex parties.
 Killing Kittens, which organizes women-led adult parties in London and New York, said Wednesday that it has secured the investment from the UK government's  Future Fund, which is designed to help startups survive the coronavirus pandemic.
 "I never envisaged Boris as a sleeping partner," said the company's CEO Emma Sayle, referring to UK Prime Minister Boris Johnson. "Killing Kittens has arrived on the digital scene and we're now pioneering the fastest-growing adult social network in the world," she added.
   Sayle founded Killing Kittens in 2005, organizing members-only masked parties in exclusive venues with a focus on "the pursuit of female pleasure," according to its website. It now has 180,000 members in 12 countries and recorded a 330% increase in traffic to its website during coronavirus lockdowns.
  The pandemic forced the company to move all its events and workshops online, accelerating existing plans to enter the  sex tech industry — a fast-growing sector that encompasses products and businesses focused on enhancing sexual experiences.
  The bulk of the UK government  funds will be spent on marketing and product innovation, including a  dating app. Killing Kittens wants to compete against dating apps such as Match.com and Bumble, which have also seen an  uptick in demand during coronavirus, and has plans to launch a digital advertising platform for lingerie and sex toy companies, Sayle said.
 "Sex tech is one of the few industries that will come away from 2020 in a much better position," said Bryony Cole, the founder of Sextech School, which teaches entrepreneurs and investors about the market. "In particular dating apps, sex toys and porn are booming during the pandemic. We are searching online for more intimacy or ways to entertain ourselves," she told CNN Business.
  Organizers of  CES,  one of the world's biggest tech trade shows, estimated roughly a dozen sex tech companies presented innovations at this year's event in Las Vegas in January.
 One of these companies was Dame, a self-described sexual wellness company, which said Wednesday that its sales have more than doubled in the first half compared with a year ago. The company made headlines last year  when it sued the New York City transit system after it rejected Dame's ads.
   Killing Kittens, which says it is valued at £11.5 million ($14.9 million) based on previous funding rounds, recently raised £170,000 ($221,780) on crowd-funding platform Seedrs. Those proceeds were matched by the Future Fund, which provides loans of between £125,000 ($162,000) and £5 million ($6.5 million) to companies, subject to at least match funding from private investors.
 More than 800 businesses have accessed the fund since its launch in May, with nearly £513 million ($664.9 million) in loans approved, according to figures from the UK Treasury. Stem & Glory, a vegan restaurant, is another of the recipients, according to the company's website.
title: 英国政府正在投资一家性派对初创公司
title_en: The British government is investing in a sex party startup

note: 这只是一件丢弃的东西。即使在那个时候，软件这个词也不是我最著名的发明。没有什么值得大书特书的(我当时只有19岁，还住在家里)。“软件”这个词不属于技术论文(此外，本科生只是主要研究人员的代笔)。那时，我在加州大学洛杉矶分校(UCLA)也有一个恶作剧的名声。同事和朋友们只是耸耸肩，毫无疑问，他们认为每一句话都是令人厌烦的恶作剧，甚至更糟，是另一个离奇的新词，我也因此而被注意到。在1953年，没有人会想到这个愚蠢的词会站稳脚跟，在几十年内，软件会进入产品和专业的通用词汇--全世界的行业都会把它作为一个庄严的名字来使用。你可以肯定的是，如果我和我的自我都怀有这样美好的幻想，那么...。然后呢？在撰写本文时，大约有420万项已授予的专利(我曾见过鲍勃·哈德森，他发明了一种沥青回收机，并因此获得了美国400万号专利--但我跑题了)。全世界肯定至少还有那么多--比如说，总数是1000万。每一项专利都有一定数量的权利要求(我最大的--嗯哼--第一个数字盒式录音机有106项权利要求)。实际上，每一项专利主张都是一项独立的发明，平均每项专利可能有10项。所以世界上肯定至少有一亿项官方认可的发明。妙语：英语只有38,000个单词。注意，发布的1亿项专利声明只正式承认了世界上一小部分的发明，但其余的都是同样的发明。发明家在将每个想法付诸实践之前不会为每个想法申请专利保护。同样，发明一个单词的人在说出或写出之前不会将其提交词典出版。与其他发明相比，单词确实很少。一个原因是它们没有商业价值，这就是为什么专利不适用于单词：你想说全息吗？--拿出你的支票簿。版权也没有意义，因为一个未复制的词根本没有语言价值。众所周知，商标保护的是买家的利益，而不是卖家的利益；因此，文字标记将使听众和读者受益，而不是演讲者和作家受益。文字是创造出来的，因此也就花光了--流传、借用、污损、破旧和遗失。辞典不像专利局那样是把关人，而仅仅是不频繁的期刊，被动地报道语言用法，必然依赖于已出版文件中的片段--在非词典中。然而，一旦一个发明的词的用法确立，词源追踪很快就会变得冷淡，如果最初的话语没有记录在案，即使是一只语言猎犬也可能无法嗅到真正发明者的气味。可怜的口头造物主，没有得到奖励，没有得到赞誉，没有得到证实。显然，发明家自己在发明东西方面比发明单词来称呼东西要好得多。自行车的踏板取自钢琴，发动机取自长号的活塞，飞机命名为机身，取名为机身，取名于尾翼，取名自箭头的尾羽。数以百万计的发明都适用于计算机，硬件和软件均有其用武之地。这款自行车的名字来自于钢琴，发动机取自长号，飞机命名为机身，取名自缝纫心轴，尾翼取自箭头的尾羽。数以百万计的发明都适用于计算机，包括硬件和软件。请读者检查名字中没有的内容是什么？和Mukashi对软件时代缺乏创造性语言的哀叹。软件时代--现在，出现了一个雄心勃勃的新词。前一句话应该加感叹号，下一句也应该加感叹号。没有软件，既不会有计算机时代，也不会有信息时代。太空时代也是如此。同时，原子时代正式结束了吗？石油时代还会持续多久？因此，软件时代有没有真正的竞争呢？好的，人们可以获得一项发明的专利，一本书的版权，一个名字的商标，但是作为一个词的发明者，一个人又能得到什么呢？如上所述，这里没有保护单词的专有工具。当然，也不会有人想要一个(如果你使用这个词，我会起诉你的，我有权利这么说)。相反，这个想法是把每一种硬币都变成货币，而不是把它成群结队地藏起来。然后孙子们长大了，是时候写一本回忆录了。这本是献给我的第一个曾孙夏洛特的。
note_en: It was just a throw-away thing.The word 'software' was hardly my most notable invention, even back then.Nothing to write home about (I was only 19 years old and still living athome).  The word 'software' did not belong in a technical paper (besides,an undergraduate is but a ghostwriter for principal researchers). Then too, I had a reputation at UCLA as a practical joker. Colleagues andfriends simply shrugged, no doubt regarding each utterance as a tiresomeprank or worse, another offbeat neologism, for which I was also becoming noted.  Nobody in 1953 would have guessed that the silly wordwould take hold, that within a few decades 'software' would enter the generalvocabulary for products and for professions -- that a worldwide industrywould wear it as a solemn name. You can be sure that if my ego and I hadharbored any such glorious visions, then... then, what?  tthe time of this writing, there are about 4.2 million issued patents (Ionce met Bob Hudson, inventor of an asphalt recycling machine for whichhe was awarded U.S. Patent No. 4,000,000 -- but I digress). There mustbe at least that many more worldwide -- a total, say, of 10 million. Everypatent has some number of "claims" (my largest -- ahem -- for the firstdigital cassette recorder has 106 claims). Each patent-claim is, in effect,a separate invention, an average of perhaps ten per patent. So there mustbe at least a hundred million officially recognized inventions in the world.Punch Line: There are only  38,000 words in theEnglish Language.  Nota bene, the hundred million issued patent-claimsofficially recognize only a small fraction of the world's inventions, butthe rest are inventions just the same. An inventor does  not applyfor patent protection for each and every idea before reducing it to practice. Likewise, a person inventing a word does  not submit it for publicationin a dictionary before speaking it or writing it.  By comparison to other inventions, words are rare indeed.One reason is that there is no commercial value to them, which is why patentsdo not apply to words: You want to say " holomorph"?-- get out your checkbook. Copyrights don't make sense either, since aword uncopied has no linguistic value at all. Trademarks, truth be known,protect the interests of buyers not sellers; accordingly, a "wordmark"would benefit listeners and readers not speakers and writers. Words are coinedand thus spent -- circulated, borrowed, defaced, worn out, and lost.  ictionariesare not gate keepers like the Patent Office but merely infrequent periodicals,passively reporting on linguistic usage, relying necessarily on snippetsin published documents -- in non-dictionaries. Once usage for an inventedword becomes established, however, the etymological trail gets cold quickly,and if the original utterance is undocumented, even a linguistic bloodhoundmay not pick up the scent of the true inventor. Pity the verbal coiner,unrewarded, unacclaimed, unattested.  Inventors themselves, it seems plain,do a whole lot better job of inventing  things than inventing wordsto call things. The bicycle got its 'pedal' from the piano, the engineappropriated 'piston' from the trombone, the airplane named its 'fuselage'from the sewing spindle and its 'empennage' from the tail feathers of anarrow.  Millions of inventions apply to computers, both hardwareand software. Readers are invited to check  What'sNot in a Name? and  Mukashi forlamentations about the dearth of inventive language in 'The Software Age'.  'The Software Age' -- now, there's an ambitious coinage.The previous sentence deserves an exclamation point and so does the nextone. Without software, there would be neither "The Computer Age" nor "TheInformation Age."  Same for "The Space Age."  Meanwhile, is "TheAtomic Age" officially over? How much longer will "The Petroleum Age" last?Accordingly, does 'The Software Age ' have any real competition?  All right, so one can get a patent on an invention, acopyright on a book, a trademark on a name, but what does one get to assertprimacy as the originator of a word? As noted above, t hereis no proprietary instrument that protects a word. Nor, of course, wouldone want one ("I'll sue you if you use that term, I have the 'wordright'on it"). On the contrary, the idea is to put each coinage into currency,not horde it away.  And then the grandkids grow up and there comes a timeto write one's memoirs.  This one is dedicated to my first great-grandchild, Charlotte.
title: 1953年10月，保罗·尼奎特创造了“软件”一词。
title_en: In October 1953 Paul Niquette coined the word 'software'

note:
飞行员们，我们感谢您选择Ars Technica来满足您的旅行需求。微软飞行模拟器的封闭测试版即将在三周后正式上市，所以我们来这里谈谈几件事，飞行前风格的问题。就当这是你的超长的安全手册吧。
首先，法国阿索博工作室(Asobo Studio)的开发者自2016年以来一直在开发这款新游戏，他们有大量关于这款游戏的新闻。我们将首先总结即将到来的功能和第三方市场合作伙伴，以及开发人员对他们自2月份游戏进入封闭阿尔法阶段以来所做的事情的看法。
其次，几个月来，我们一直在测试MSFS的Closed Alpha，尽管我们使用了一系列可笑的视觉水印，阻止了我们泄露全球每一次美丽飞行的镜头。然而，这个alpha版本与我在2019年8月测试的版本非常相似，这意味着在大约两周前发布更全面的更新之前，我没有太多要报告的内容。
从技术上讲，这并不是审查，因为在8月18日针对Windows10个人电脑发布之前，内容将经过进一步的优化、修复和润色。事实上，发射只是一个开始。MSF一再被描述为一个为期十年的项目，这种项目的内容将在每一步都会成长和成熟。当被追问零售上市日期时，MSFS负责人约格·诺伊曼(Jorg Neumann)表示：这不是一个一次性的产品。这是一次旅行。但它绝对是一个完整的产品。
也就是说，就内容、结构和图形而言，即将进入封闭测试版(并在未来几周占据你最喜欢的、沉迷于飞行的YouTube频道)的MSFS版本，几乎就是你可以期待在几周内上市的版本，这给了我们很多可以在这部长篇实践中谈论的话题。
然而：微软和Asobo显然对开发计划敞开了大门，所以今天的游戏可能会与我们六个月后甚至更长时间内看到的有很大不同。
如果你是2020年版MSFS的新手，你可以了解我2019年9月关于这款游戏的专题报道。简而言之：MSFS可以让你真实地飞越我们整个星球，完成每个已知的机场作为起飞和降落的目的地，因为持续连接到Azure云服务上提供的大约2PB(200万GB)的Bing地图数据。在紧要关头，你可以离线玩游戏，在紧要关头仍然可以享受一个模糊但完整的地球-或者在离线之前预先下载首选的飞行目的地。
8月份发布的版本将只在Windows PC上发布，尽管微软此前曾宣布打算在晚些时候将MSFS引入Xbox游戏机，但这一晚些时候还没有敲定。这两家公司都没有决定到底哪些Xbox游戏机可以与这款游戏配合使用。
尽管缺乏新闻，我们还是得到了一些关于PC生态系统支持的耐人寻味的澄清，这是粉丝们一直在叫嚣的：
是的，MSFS将在Steam上发射-而且它不会面临延迟。你现在就可以在Steam上预订所有版本的MSFS软件，该版本将在其Windows应用商店版本(8月18日)当天解锁。
是的，如果你付费购买Xbox游戏通行证，你将能够购买MSFS豪华版或高级版的升级版，因为你已经可以使用游戏的基础版本(否则零售价为60美元)。
是的，MSFS将支持TrackIR的动作感应系统，在本文上线的前几天，TrackIR测试版已经进入了游戏的测试版本。(之前的声明暗示TrackIR的实施将需要更长的时间，所以我们很高兴看到它在封闭测试期及时落地。)。
最后，这个功能值得鼓点...。是的，MSFS将支持虚拟现实耳机。更重要的是，VR切换将是完全免费的，不需要额外购买。
考虑到MSFS的非VR版本在CPU和GPU上的要求已经很高，我们很惊讶地看到，在其他可能的功能之前宣布支持VR，特别是支持Xbox控制台。(我稍后会讲到这一点。)。目前，Asobo已经将这款游戏的虚拟现实版放在了一个有点模糊的2020年秋季发布窗口，而这一消息确实伴随着一个问题：在发布时，这种模式只适用于即将推出的惠普混响G2系统。
当被追问时，Neumann和MSFS团队的其他成员拒绝具体说明他们的VR实施可能需要限制单个PC-VR系统的具体内容，特别是因为市场上的每一款耳机都依赖OpenVR呼叫进行轴定位。(事实上，这正是非Oculus耳机被骗加载Oculus独家软件的原因。)。混响G2还恰好借用了Valve Index的镜头阵列的工程和设计。因此，我们希望这款游戏的虚拟现实模式在发布后不久就能去除这些设备限制标志，并支持其他耳机。
该团队也没有准备好澄清手控控制器在游戏的虚拟现实版本中是如何工作的。Asobo首席技术官David Dedeine说，这是一个迭代的过程。我们正在努力寻找一种通用的东西，无论你使用什么设备，它都可以工作。我要说的是，我们有使用先进控制的有趣的原型。有激动人心的事情在发生。我已经对团队为了改善与虚拟现实中驾驶舱的互动所做的事情感到惊讶。这和用鼠标互动是不同的。我们已经体验到的东西非常精彩，我们将把它应用到当前版本的游戏中。
如果你到目前为止已经看过这款游戏的镜头，你可能会想知道它详细的视觉效果和巨大的视野将如何转化为虚拟现实的要求-就像两个高分辨率显示器(每只眼睛一个)以90 Hz的刷新率运行。Asobo坚持认为，VR模式的目标应该是接近游戏的中等图形预设，根据我的经验，这是相当高的分辨率：足够蓬松的云层，相对密集的风景。该团队表示，这种图形保真度归根结底在于高效的CPU渲染(计算右眼的方式，然后将大部分右眼移动到左眼)，以及低功耗Hololens耳机的专业技能。Dedeine说，Hololens是一种移动设备，(我们的项目)迫使我们进行终极优化。所以我们的团队在这个问题上是即插即用的。
note_en:
Pilots, we thank you for choosing Ars Technica for your travel needs.  Microsoft Flight Simulator's closed beta is about to take off as a prelude to its retail launch in three weeks, so we're here to talk about a few things, preflight style. Consider this your incredibly long safety manual.
 First, the developers at France's Asobo Studio, who have been building this new game since 2016, have a  ton of news about the game. We'll start by summing up upcoming features and third-party marketplace partners, along with the devs' perspective about what they've done since the game entered a closed alpha phase in February.
 Second, we've been testing  MSFS's closed alpha for months, albeit with a ridiculous series of visual watermarks that has stopped us from leaking footage of every beautiful flight across the globe. That alpha build was quite similar to  what I tested in August 2019, however, which meant I didn't have much to report until a fuller update that landed roughly two weeks ago.
 This technically isn't a "review," since the content will see further refinements, fixes, and polishing passes ahead of its August 18 launch for Windows 10 PCs. Indeed, the launch is only the beginning.  MSFS has been repeatedly described as a "ten-year" project, the kind whose content will grow and mature every step of the way. When pressed about the retail launch date,  MSFS head Jorg Neumann said the following: "It's not a one-and-done product. It's a journey. But it's definitely a complete product."
 Which is to say: In terms of content, structure, and graphics, the version of  MSFS that's about to enter closed beta (and dominate your favorite flight-obsessed YouTube channels for the coming weeks) is pretty much what you can expect to go on sale in a few weeks, which gives us plenty to talk about in this feature-length hands-on.
 And yet: Microsoft and Asobo have clearly left the development plan wide open, so the game today will likely vary quite a bit from what we might see in six months and beyond.
      If you're new to the 2020 version of  MSFS, you can catch up on  my September 2019 feature about the game. Short version:  MSFS lets you realistically fly across our entire planet, complete with every known airport as a destination for takeoffs and landings, as supercharged by a constant connection to roughly 2 petabytes (2,000,000 GB) of Bing map data served on Azure Cloud Services. In a pinch, you can play the game offline and still enjoy a blurry-but-complete Earth in a pinch—or pre-download preferred flying destinations before going offline.
 The version launching in August will land exclusively on Windows PCs, and while Microsoft has previously announced its intent to bring  MSFS to Xbox consoles at a later date, that "later date" still hasn't been firmed up. Neither has any decision about exactly which Xbox consoles will work with the game.
 Despite that lack of news, we've gotten some intriguing clarifications about PC ecosystem support that fans have been clamoring for:
 Yes,  MSFS will launch on Steam—and it won't face a delay. You can pre-order  every version of  MSFS on Steam right now, and that version will unlock the same day as its Windows Store version (August 18).
 Yes, if you pay for Xbox Game Pass, you will be able to buy an upgrade to the Deluxe or Premium versions of  MSFS, since you'll already have access to the game's base version (which otherwise retails for $60).
 Yes,  MSFS will support  TrackIR's motion-sensing system, and the TrackIR beta implementation went into the game's test version a few days before this article went live. (Previous statements suggested that TrackIR implementation would take longer, so we're happy to see it land in time for the closed beta period.)
  Finally, this feature deserves a drum roll... yes,  MSFS will support virtual reality headsets. What's more, the VR toggle will be entirely free, with no additional purchase required.
 We're surprised to see VR support announced ahead of other possible features, particularly Xbox console support, given that  MSFS's non-VR version is already so demanding on CPUs and GPUs. (Which I'll get to in a bit.) For now, Asobo has put the game's VR version into a somewhat nebulous "fall 2020" release window, and the news does come with one catch: at launch, this mode will only work with  the upcoming HP Reverb G2 system.
 When pressed, Neumann and the rest of the  MSFS team declined to specify exactly what about their VR implementation might require a limit to a single PC-VR system, especially since every headset on the market relies on OpenVR calls for axis orientation. (Indeed, this is exactly  why non-Oculus headsets can be tricked into loading Oculus-exclusive software.) The Reverb G2 also happens to borrow the engineering and design of the  Valve Index's lens array. Thus, we're hopeful that the game's VR mode removes these device-limiting flags and supports other headsets shortly after its launch.
 The team also wasn't ready to clarify how hand-tracked controllers might work within the game's VR version. "It's an iterative process," Asobo CCO David Dedeine said. "We're trying to find something universal, that will work no matter what device you're using. We have interesting prototypes using advanced controls, I will say. There are exciting things happening. I am already amazed at what the team has made, in order to improve interactions with the cockpit in VR. It's different than interacting with a mouse. What we've already experienced is brilliant and will inform the current version of the game."
 If you've seen the game's footage thus far, you may wonder how its detailed visuals and massive vistas will translate to the demands of VR—as in, two high-resolution displays (one for each eye) running at a 90Hz refresh rate. Asobo insists that the VR mode should target something close to the game's "medium" graphics preset, which, in my experience, is quite high-res: sufficiently fluffy clouds, relatively dense landscapes. The team says this graphical fidelity boils down to things like efficient CPU rendering ("the way you compute the right eye, then move much of that to the left eye") and expertise with the low-powered Hololens headset. "Hololens is a mobility device, and [our projects for that] forced us to ultimate optimization," Dedeine said. "So our team is plug-and-play on this subject."
title: 飞行模拟器小姐：我们的新功能，华丽的飞行
title_en: MS Flight Simulator: Our yoke-on look at new features, gorgeous flights

note:
然而，这些美好的感觉受到了冠状病毒大流行的沉重打击。自从新冠肺炎抵达欧洲以来，Ecosia的月度收入已经减少了一半，营业额从2月份的260万欧元下降到6月份的140万欧元。它担心每0.8秒种一棵树的承诺面临风险。
由于抵制Android OS制造商谷歌(Google)今年全年一直在运行的拍卖过程，该公司还受到了地区知名度的打击，这是对2018年欧盟委员会(Commission)的一项反垄断裁决的回应，该裁决发现，这家科技巨头在运营智能手机平台的方式上违反了欧盟竞争规则-包括通过对手机制造商施加条件，要求其在设备默认情况下预装自己的服务(如谷歌搜索(Google Search))。
在欧盟委员会的决定之后，谷歌开始向欧洲的Android用户显示搜索“选择屏幕”，现在的拍卖过程决定了哪些竞争对手的搜索引擎出现在搜索“选择屏幕”上。目前，谷歌通过拍卖向非谷歌搜索引擎提供了三个付费位置。安装新设备的Android用户总是将谷歌自己的搜索引擎视为四个选项之一。
这家科技巨头的竞争对手一直辩称，这种“付费游戏”模式并不能弥补其在安卓(Android)系统上的反竞争行为。安卓是世界上占主导地位的智能手机操作系统。尽管大多数人(包括DuckDuckGo)从一开始就感到被迫参与其拍卖过程。放弃进入Android搜索市场这条最重要的路线并不是大多数企业都能负担得起的奢侈品。
Ecosia是一家非营利性组织，是最后一个主要的坚持。但现在它表示，为了保持在该地区的竞争力，它被迫结束了抵制活动。这意味着它将参加下一轮Android选择屏幕的拍卖-计划在第四季度初。如果它赢得了每个国家的任何一个位置，它将在未来作为搜索选项出现在这些Android用户面前，尽管考虑到拍卖过程的长度，可能要到明年才会出现。
它仍然强烈批评谷歌的付费播放模式，辩称这不是欧盟委员会认定的违反反垄断行为的补救措施。它还哀叹，鉴于所有相反的证据，欧盟立法者正在采取“观望”的方式来确定谷歌的“补救措施”是否真的恢复了竞争。
Ecosia首席执行官克里斯蒂安·克罗尔通过视频聊天对TechCrunch说：“我们抵制拍卖的主要原因是我们认为这是非常不公平和反竞争的。”“我们不仅认为公平竞争不应该在拍卖中被出售，而且拍卖的设计方式也基本上确保了只有最不感兴趣的选项才能获胜。
“由于我们的商业模式是将大部分收入用于植树，我们基本上不可能真的在拍卖模式中获胜。如果你已经是一个搜索引擎，那就相当有名了，…。那么通过这个屏幕你会有很多吃人的效果。所以我们基本上是在为流量付费，不管怎么说，我们都是免费获得的，…。所以这是超级不公平和反竞争的。“
克罗尔对欧盟委员会没有立即拒绝谷歌的选择屏幕拍卖模式表示强烈的惊讶-他说，他们似乎没有从欧盟早些时候反对微软将其Internet Explorer浏览器与其占主导地位的桌面操作系统Windows捆绑在一起的干预中学到任何东西。(在这种情况下，在微软同意实施投票屏幕，提供多达12种浏览器选择后，这一传奇故事就结束了，这为谷歌后来与自己的Chrome浏览器获得份额铺平了道路。)。
在去年短暂的初期，谷歌确实在欧洲提供了免费的选择屏幕，将其推广到现有的Android设备上-搜索竞争对手的选择是根据它们在每个国家的市场受欢迎程度(在一些市场，包括Ecosia)。
然而，这家科技巨头当时表示，随着时间的推移，它将“演变”其实施方式。几个月后，一种拍卖模式被宣布为新的Android设备的到来--“付费播放”的方式在今年年初开始。
包括DuckDuckGo和Qwant在内的搜索竞争对手立即叫嚣犯规。然而，欧盟委员会的反应一直是踢罐子-监管机构提供了陈词滥调，称他们将“密切监控”。他们还声称“致力于全面有效地执行该决定”。
然而，那句话中遗漏的形容词是“快”。谷歌的竞争对手会争辩说，要想补救措施有效，它需要非常快地发生，就像现在-或者，对他们中的一些人来说，风险真的是破产了。毕竟，欧盟委员会的Android反垄断决定(是的，谷歌正在上诉)已经可以追溯到整整两年前。
克罗尔说：“我发现欧盟委员会从一开始就没有拒绝(谷歌的拍卖模式)，因为微软案中选择屏成功的一些关键原则完全被忽视了，谷歌把整个选择屏的概念变成了他们的优势，这让他们感到非常惊讶。”“我们甚至没有在内部称它为‘选择屏幕’，我们只是称它为‘拍卖屏幕’。既然我们现在停下来抵制，我们称之为‘别无选择的屏幕’。“。
“这是谷歌给人一种有自由选择但没有自由选择的印象，”他补充道。“如果谷歌的目标是为用户提供选择，那么他们会提供最有趣的选择，那就是市场占有率最高的搜索引擎--所以肯定是我们，DuckDuckGo，也许还有其他一些参与者。”但这不是他们想要做的。“。
克罗尔指出，谷歌的另一个德国搜索竞争对手Cliqz不得不在今年年初停止其反跟踪替代方案-这意味着现在谷歌的本土反跟踪竞争对手少了一个。虽然Ecosia觉得自己别无选择，只能参与谷歌的拍卖游戏，但克罗尔表示，它也不知道参与是否会导致Ecosia向谷歌支付过高的价格购买线索，这意味着它产生的收入较少，无法种植那么多树…。或者，嗯，任何一棵树，以防最坏的情况发生。
(注：克罗尔在签署谷歌要求拍卖参与者签署的保密协议之前接受了TechCrunch的采访，该协议对他们一旦参与拍卖可以说的话施加了法律限制-这反过来又是一个问题，另一家欧洲搜索竞争对手Qwant也抱怨不公平的…。)。
“除了参与之外，我们别无选择，”克罗尔补充说。“因为我们想要进入Android平台。因此，基本上谷歌已经成功地胁迫每个人按照自己的规则玩-这是一场游戏，谷歌不仅是裁判，他们还获得了一张免费的门票，他们也是玩家…。
“不知何故，谷歌神奇地说服了公众，但我认为欧盟委员会也认为，他们需要在拍卖中创造收入，因为他们通过Android开发等方式有太多成本。当然，他们有成本…是真的。但他们也通过与设备制造商达成的交易获得了巨额利润，而这些利润根本没有被分享。“。
Kroll指出，谷歌每年支付(据报道)120亿美元，成为苹果iOS平台Safari的默认搜索引擎--尽管谷歌通过Android免费进入绝大多数移动搜索者的眼球(谷歌对Chrome也是如此)。(据报道，谷歌每年支付120亿美元，成为苹果iOS平台Safari的默认搜索引擎)--尽管谷歌通过Android免费进入绝大多数移动搜索者的眼球(Chrome也是如此)。
“如果他们愿意为这些平台支付同样多的钱，他们很快就会破产，”他辩称。“所以他们免费获得了所有这些，还免费获得了其他好处--比如预装了Play Store，比如预装了谷歌地图，预装了YouTube等等--这些都是收入来源。”但他们没有分享这些收入中的任何一项。他们只是试图将他们拥有的所有成本都外包给竞争对手，我认为这是非常不公平的。“。
虽然谷歌的母公司Alphabet在报告其财务状况时，并没有特别列出Google Play的收入，但SensorTower的数据显示，在这六个月的时间里，仅Play Store的收入就达到了173亿美元，同比增长21%。而Play只是谷歌通过“免费”安卓获得的摇钱树之一。
自欧盟委员会2018年做出针对Android的反垄断裁决以来，克罗尔辩称，对于像Ecosia这样的搜索竞争对手来说，一切都没有改变，这些竞争对手正试图为消费者提供更有趣的点击量价值交换。
“谷歌做得非常成功的是，他们只是准时上场，”他建议说。“我们的竞争对手Cliqz已经因此破产了。因此，这一策略似乎对谷歌非常有效。我们也不能失去对这些平台…的访问。我真的希望欧盟执委会真的能在这方面做点什么，因为在微软案中已经成功做到了，我们只需要完全一样的做法。“
Kroll还提到了DuckDuckGo关于“公平选择屏幕”的设计建议--我们去年在这里谈到了这一点，但谷歌(和欧盟委员会)到目前为止一直对此置之不理。
他怀疑监管机构正在等待，看看未来一年或更长时间市场会是什么样子。但当然，到那时，要挽救更多替代搜索引擎免于Cliqz式的灭亡，从而进一步巩固谷歌的地位，可能已经为时已晚。这显然与反垄断补救措施相反。
欧盟专员玛格丽特·维斯特格(Margrethe Vestager)去年已经承认，她对这家科技巨头的另一项干预-谷歌广告感知(Google AdSense)反垄断案-是“执法没有成功的一个例子，因为它未能恢复竞争”。因此，如果她一不小心，她在失败补救措施上的记录可能会损害她作为至少愿意与科技巨头较量的反垄断负责人的高调声誉。在竞争方面，它必须都是关于结果的-或者你甚至作为所谓的执法者在做什么？
克罗尔补充道：“我一直担心，当大企业比我们的公共机构更强大时，这一点可能会到来，我在想，这一点是否还没有达到。”他假设，目前尚不清楚欧盟-作为一个目前面临着大量自身问题的经济和政治项目-是否有足够的弹性，能够在不久的将来执行自己的竞争法。因此，他真正的关键点是：如果不是现在，什么时候？(或者，好吧，怎么做？)。
诚然，欧盟委员会围绕竞争政策和数字市场所说的话与其在如何补救市场失灵方面的不插手做法之间确实存在越来越大的脱节-在数字市场上，欧盟委员会清楚地意识到，如果要防止垄断势力不可逆转地颠覆这些市场，监管干预需要能够更快地采取行动(它目前正在就是否赋予自己更大的干预权进行咨询)。没有有效的补救措施就没有有效的执行。因此，在做出决定后丢掉球，真的会让整个行动失败。
维斯特格清楚地认识到数字背景下存在一个问题--去年他告诉欧盟议会：“我们必须考虑影响更深远的补救措施。”(尽管，仍然没有承诺有更深远的补救措施。)。然而，与此同时，她鼓吹“观望”作为她的首要哲学-一种政策“推拉”，似乎阻碍了该部门甚至无法娱乐地承担起更灵活、更积极和更迭代的角色，支持市场真正恢复竞争。至少在进行冗长的咨询工作之前不会，这会进一步挑起事端，
如果欧盟立法者不能从他们自己相对较近的数字反垄断历史中吸取教训(微软将IE与Windows捆绑在一起)，以有效执行一个非常简单类似的反垄断案件(谷歌将Search&amp；的其他服务与Android捆绑在一起)，那么你就不得不质疑，他们为什么认为现在需要新的反垄断工具来恰当地解决数字垄断问题。考虑到他们似乎不能有效地使用他们已经拥有的工具。
面对平台权力的变动，目前的欧盟监管机构看起来确实越来越像是失去了信心-和/或沦为避险情绪的牺牲品。(也就是说，有传言称，欧盟委员会正准备批准谷歌对Fitbit的收购，尽管谷歌做出了纸面上的承诺，尽管人们对隐私和加强数据整合提出了重大担忧--如果这是真的，这将再次意味着欧盟委员会忽视了自己最近天真地接受其他类似科技巨头指控的历史。)
“我的感觉是，在微软的Case…案中发生了什么。委员会里只有一个人疯了，他说这就是决定，你必须这么做…。也许这只需要这些勇气。那么这可能是一个政治问题。维斯特格真的愿意挑起这些战斗吗？“。克罗尔问道。
“我的感觉是，如果人们真的了解情况，他们就会关心，但你实际上需要做一点解释，说明在搜索这样一个重要领域拥有一家占主导地位的公司是不好的，这基本上是关闭了其他所有人的市场。”
当被问及他对目前积极关注围绕谷歌-甚至是大部分大型科技公司-的反垄断担忧的美国立法者有什么信息时，克罗尔说：“我是竞争的粉丝，我也钦佩谷歌；我认为谷歌是一家非常聪明的公司，但我认为已经到了权力过于集中，对社会造成危险的地步。”…。谷歌在各个领域的主导地位让我们备受煎熬。谷歌正在做的一些事情显然是反竞争的。
他建议监管机构密切关注的一件具体事情是，谷歌支付给苹果多少钱，才能成为Safari的默认搜索选项。“为了赢得Safari的搜索量，它支付的钱超出了它的实际承受能力--我认为这是非常反竞争的，”他辩称。“他们已经拥有三分之二的市场份额，他们基本上把剩下的东西都买下来，这样他们就可以巩固自己的主导地位。
“监管机构应该密切关注这一点，今后不允许谷歌参与任何其他浏览器的违约竞标。”我认为这甚至会对浏览器有利，因为从长远来看，最终会再次出现争夺这些位置的竞争。目前，谷歌之所以能赢下这场官司，是因为他们的选择越来越少，其他搜索提供商也没有多少可供选择的了。
他还辩称，他们需要让谷歌修复“他们造成的一些损害”--即由于不公平地获得市场份额而造成的--执行他所说的“真正公平的选择屏幕”；无偿并基于与用户的相关性。通过在Android和Chrome设备上这样做。
“我认为直到一年前，如果你使用Safari浏览器或Firefox浏览器访问Google.com，那么谷歌会建议你安装Chrome。对我来说，这显然是滥用一种主导地位来支持公司的另一部分，“他辩称。“谷歌需要修复这一点，这需要非常迅速地进行--否则其他公司可能会(倒闭)。”
“我们仍然做得不错，但我们受到了日冕的沉重打击，我们的收入出现了巨大的损失。其他公司可能会受到更严重的打击，我不知道。而且我们没有大公司那么雄厚的财力。因此，如果不尽快采取行动，其他公司可能会消失。“他补充道。
谷歌的一位发言人向我们介绍了关于拍卖的常见问题。在不能直接引用的进一步言论中，他们声称拍卖是确定如何填补空缺的一种公平和客观的方法，并补充说，拍卖产生的收入有助于谷歌继续投资于开发和维护Android。
虽然欧盟委员会的一位女发言人告诉我们，它一直在与谷歌“讨论”选择屏幕机制，但她形容这是“来自市场的相关反馈，特别是关于选择屏幕的呈现和机制以及竞争对手搜索提供商的选择机制”。
这位女发言人还重申了早些时候的评论，委员会正在继续监测谷歌选择屏幕的实施情况，并“致力于全面和有效地执行这一决定”。
然而，一位知情人士表示，欧盟立法者认为，几美分的有偿溢价配售远远优于谷歌之前向竞争对手提供的产品-即根本没有可见性-因此他们认为，有总比没有好。

However these good feels have been hit hard by the coronavirus pandemic.  Ecosia   has seen its monthly revenues slashed by half since COVID-19 arrived in Europe, with turnover falling from €2.6M in February to just €1.4M in June. It’s worried that its promise of planting a tree every 0.8 seconds is at risk.
 It has also suffered a knock to regional visibility as a result of boycotting an auction process that  Android   OS maker Google has been running throughout this year, as a response to a 2018 Commission antitrust decision that found the tech giant had violated EU competition rules in how it operates the smartphone platform — including via conditions placed on phone makers to pre-load its own services (like Google search) as device defaults.
  An auction process now determines which rival search engines appear on  a search ‘choice screen’ Google began showing to Android users in Europe in the wake of the Commission decision. Currently, Google offers three paid slots via the auction to non-Google search engines. Android users setting up a new device always see Google’s own search engine as one of the four total options.
 The tech giant’s rivals have consistently argued this  ‘pay to play’ model is no remedy for its anti-competitive behavior with Android, the world’s dominant smartphone OS. Although most (including  DuckDuckGo) felt forced to participate in its auction process from the get-go. Forgoing the most prominent route to the Android search market isn’t exactly a luxury most businesses could afford.
 Ecosia, a not-for-profit, was the last major hold out. But now it says it’s been forced  to end its boycott in a bid to remain competitive in the region. This means it will participate in the next auction round for the Android choice screen — scheduled for the beginning of Q4. If it wins any per country slots it will appear as a search choice option to those Android users in future, though likely not til next year given the length of the auction process.
 It remains highly critical of Google’s pay-to-play model, arguing it’s no remedy for the antitrust violations identified by the Commission. It also laments that EU lawmakers are taking a ‘wait and see’ approach to determining whether Google’s ‘remedy’ is actually restoring competition, given all the evidence to the contrary.
 “The main reason why we boycotted the auction is because we think it’s highly unfair and anticompetitive,” says Ecosia CEO Christian Kroll, speaking to TechCrunch via video chat. “Not only do we think that fair competition shouldn’t be sold off in an auction but also the way the auction is designed basically makes sure that only the least interesting options can win.
 “Since we have a business model where we use most of our revenues to plant trees we basically can’t really win in an auction model. If you’re already a search engine that’s quite well known… then you have a lot of cannibalization effects through this screen. So we’re basically paying for traffic that we would get for free anyway… So it’s just super unfair and anticompetitive.”
 Kroll expresses emphatic surprise that the Commission didn’t immediately reject Google’s auction model for the choice screen — saying it seems as if they’ve learned nothing from the EU’s  earlier intervention against Microsoft’s tying of its Internet Explorer browser with its dominant desktop OS, Windows. (In that case the saga ended after  Microsoft   agreed to implement a ballot screen offering a choice of up to 12 browsers, which paved the road for Google to later gain share with its own Chrome browser.)
 For a brief initial period last year Google did offer a fee-less choice screen in Europe, pushing this out to existing Android devices — with search rivals selected  based on their market popularity per country (which, in some markets, included Ecosia).
 However the tech giant said then that it would be “evolving” its implementation over time. And a  few months later an auction model was announced as incoming for new Android devices — with that ‘pay-to-play’ approach kicking off at the start of this year.
 Search rivals including  DuckDuckGo   and Qwant  immediately cried foul. Yet the response from the Commission has been to kick the can — with regulators offering platitudes that said they would “closely monitor”. They also claimed to be “committed to a full and effective implementation of the decision”.
 However the missing adjective in that statement is ‘fast’.  Google rivals would argue that for a remedy to be effective it needs to happen really fast, like  now  — or, for some of them, the risk really is going out of business. After all, the Commission’s Android antitrust decision (which, yes,  Google is appealing)  already  dates back two full years .
 “I find it very surprising that the  European Commission   hasn’t rejected [Google’s auction model] from the start because some of the key principles from what made the choice screen successful in the Microsoft case have just been completely disregarded and been turned around by Google to turn the whole concept of a choice screen to their advantage,” says Kroll. “We’re not even calling it the ‘choice screen’ internally, we just call it the ‘auction screen’. And since we’re now stopping to boycott we call it the ‘no choice screen’.”
 “It’s Google’s way to give the impression that there’s free choice but there is no free choice,” he adds. “If Google’s objective here would be to create choice for the user then they would present the most interesting options, which are the search engines with the highest marketshares — so definitely us, DuckDuckGo and maybe some other players as well. But that’s not what they’re trying to do.”
 Kroll points out that another German search rival to Google, Cliqz, had to pull the plug on its anti-tracking alternative at the  start of this year — meaning there’s now one less homegrown anti-tracking rival to Google in play. And while Ecosia feels it has no choice but to participate in Google’s auction game Kroll says it also can’t know whether or not participating will result in Ecosia overpaying Google for leads that then mean it generates less revenue and can’t plant as many trees… Or, well, any trees if the worst were to happen.
 (NB: Kroll was speaking to TechCrunch ahead of signing an NDA that Google requires participants of the auction to sign which puts a legal limit on what they can say about the process once they’re involved — which, in turn, is a problematic element that another European search rival, Qwant, has also  complained is unfair… )
 “We don’t have any choice left, other than to participate,” adds Kroll. “Because we want to have access to the Android platform. So basically Google has successfully bullied everyone to play to its own rules — and it’s a game where Google is not only the referee but also they get a free ticket and they are also players…
 “Somehow Google magically convinced the public but I think also the European Commission that they need to generate revenue in an auction because they have so many costs through the Android development and so on. It is of course true that they have costs… but they are also generating massive profit through the deals that they then make with the device makers and those profits are not at all shared.”
 Kroll points out that Google shells out a ( reported) $12BN per year to be the default search engine in Safari on Apple’s iOS platform — even as it pays nothing to get in front of the vast majority of mobile searchers’ eyeballs via Android (and does the same with Chrome).
 “If they would pay the same amount of money for those platform they would soon be bankrupt,” he argues. “So they are getting all this for free and they are also getting other benefits for free — like having the Play Store preinstalled, like having Google Maps preinstalled, YouTube preinstalled and so on — which are all revenue sources. But they’re not sharing any of those revenue. They just try to outsource all of the costs that they have to their competitors, which is I think very unfair.”
 While  Alphabet,   Google’s parent entity, doesn’t break out Google Play revenue specifically from within a generic “advertising” bucket when it reports its financials, data from  SensorTower for the first half of 2020 suggests it generated $17.3BN in Play Store revenue alone over this six-month period, up 21% year-over-year. And Play is just one of the moneyspinners Google derives via ‘free’ Android.
 Since the Commission’s antitrust 2018 decision against Android Kroll argues that nothing has changed for search competitors like Ecosia which are trying to offer consumers a more interesting value exchange for their clicks.
 “What Google is doing very successfully is they’re just playing on time,” he suggests. “Our competitor, Cliqz, already went bankrupt because of that. So the strategy seems to work really well for Google. And we also can’t afford to lose access to those platforms… I really hope that the European Commission will actually do something about this because it has been done successfully in the Microsoft case and we just need exactly the same.”
 Kroll also flags DuckDuckGo’s design suggestions for “a fair choice screen” — which  we covered here last year but which Google (and the Commission) have so far simply ignored.
 He suspects regulators are waiting to see how the market looks in another year or more. But of course by then it may be too late to save more alternative search engines from a Cliqz-style demise, thereby further strengthening Google’s position. Which would obviously be the opposite of an antitrust remedy.
 Commissioner  Margrethe Vestager   already conceded  last year that another of her interventions against the tech giant — the  Google AdSense antitrust case — is an example of “enforcement that hasn’t succeeded because it has failed to restore competition”. So if she’s not careful her record on failed remedies could dent her high profile reputation for being an antitrust chief who’s at least willing to take on tech giants. Where competition is concerned, it must be all about outcomes — or what are you even doing as claimed law ‘enforcers’?
 “I always fear that the point might come when big corporates are more powerful than our public institutions and I’m wondering if this point isn’t already reached,” adds Kroll, positing that it’s not clear whether the EU — as an economic and political project now facing plenty of its own issues — will have enough resilience to be able to enforce its own competition law in the near future. So really his key point is: If not now, when? (Or, well, how?)
 It’s certainly true that there’s a growing disconnect between what the Commission is  saying around competition policy and digital markets — where it’s alive to the critique that regulatory interventions need to be able to move much faster if they’re to prevent monopoly power irreversibly tipping these markets (it’s  currently consulting on whether to give itself greater powers of intervention) — and its hands-off approach to how to remedy market failure. tl;dr there’s no effective enforcement without effective remedies. So dropping the ball after the fact of a decision really defeats the whole operation.
 Vestager clearly recognizes there’s a problem in the digital context — telling the EU parliament last year: “We have to consider remedies that are much more far reaching”. (Albeit, still not  committing to having much more far reaching remedies.) Yet in parallel she  preaches ‘wait and see’ as her overarching philosophy — a policy ‘push-pull’ which seems to be preventing the unit from even entertaining taking on a more agile, active and iterative role in supporting markets towards actual restoration of competition. At least not before a lengthy consultation exercise which further kicks the can,
 If EU lawmakers can’t learn the lessons from their own relatively recent digital antitrust history (Microsoft tying IE to Windows) to effectively enforce what is a pretty straightforwardly similar antitrust case (Google tying search & its other services to Android), you have to question why they think they need new antitrust tools to properly tackle digital monopolies now. Given they don’t seem able to effectively wield the tools they’ve already got.
 It does rather look increasingly like the current crop of EU regulators have lost conviction — and/or fallen prey to risk aversion — in the face of platform power moves. (To wit: There are whispers the Commission is preparing to wave through  Google’s acquisition of Fitbit, on  paper-thin promises from Google, despite  major concerns raised about privacy and increased data consolidation — which, if true, would again mean the Commission  ignoring its own recent history of naively swallowing other similar tech giant claims.)
 “My feeling is, what has happened in the Microsoft case… there was just somebody in the Commission crazy enough to say this is what the decision is and you have to do it… And maybe it just takes those kind of guts. That’s then maybe a political question. Is Vestager willing to really pick those battles?” asks Kroll.
 “My feeling is if people really understand the situation then they would care but you actually need to do a little bit of explaining that it’s not good to have a dominant player that is in such an important sector like search, and that is basically shutting down the market for everybody else.”
 Asked what his message is for the US lawmakers now actively eyeing antitrust concerns around Google — and indeed  much of big tech — Kroll says: “I’m a fan of competition and I also admire Google; I think Google is a very clever company but I think there is a point reached where there’s so much concentration of power that it gets dangerous for society…  We’ve been suffering quite a lot from all the dominance that Google has in the various sectors. There are just things that Google are doing that are obviously anticompetitive.”
 One specific thing he suggests regulators take a close look at is how much money Google pays Apple to be the default search option on Safari.  “It’s paying more money than it can actually afford to win the Safari search volume — that I think is very anticompetitive,” he argues. “They already own two-thirds of the market and they basically buy whatever’s left over so that they can just cement their dominance.
 “The regulators should have a very close look at that and disallow Google to participate in any of those bids for default positions in other browsers in the future. I think that would even be beneficial for browsers because in the long term there would finally be competition for those spots again. Currently Google’s just winning them because they’re running out of options and there are not many other search providers left to choose from.”
 He also argues they need to make Google repair “ some of the damage they’ve done” — i.e. as a result of unfairly gaining marketshare — by enforcing what he calls “a really fair choice screen”; non-paid and based on relevance for users. And by doing so on Android and Chrome devices.
 “I think until a year ago if you visited Google.com with your Safari browser or Firefox browser then Google would recommend to install Chrome. And for me that’s a clear abuse of one dominant position to support another part of your company,” he argues. “Google needs to repair that and that needs to happen very quickly — because otherwise other companies might [go out of business].”
 “We’re still doing okay but we have been hit heavily by corona and we have a huge loss in revenue. Other companies might be hit even worse, I don’t know. And we don’t have the same deep pockets that the big players have. So other companies might disappear if nothing’s done soon,” he adds.
  A Google spokesperson pointed us to its  FAQ about the auction. In further remarks which they specified could not be directly quoted they claimed an auction is a fair and objective method of determining how to fill available slots, adding that the revenue generated via the auction helps Google continue to invest in developing and maintaining Android.
 While a spokeswoman for the Commission told us it has been “discussing” the choice screen mechanism with Google, following what she described as “relevant feedback from the market, in particular in relation to the presentation and mechanics of the choice screen and to the selection mechanism of rival search providers”.
 The spokeswoman also reiterated earlier comments, that the Commission is continuing to monitor Google’s choice screen implementation and is “committed to a full and effective implementation of the decision”.
 However a source familiar with the matter said EU lawmakers view paid premium placement for a few cents as far superior to what Google was offering rivals before — i.e. no visibility at all — and thus take the view that that something is better than nothing.
title: Ecosia称，谷歌在Android上的“别无选择”屏幕不起作用-质疑欧盟的反垄断执法方式
title_en: Google’s “no choice” screen on Android isn’t working, says Ecosia — querying the EU’s approach to antitrust enforcement

但是，新的飞行模拟器还在很大程度上仍在进行中，甚至距离预定的8月18日发射日期只有几周的时间。它还处于正式的测试阶段，所以至少还有时间来解决我下面列出的一些问题。因为微软和负责模拟器开发的Asobo Studios在Azure中使用微软的AI技术，根据微软的必应地图数据自动生成大部分风景，你会发现世界上有很多奇怪的地方。跑道中央有滑行道灯，小型私人田野上有巨大的机库和机组人员大巴，在机场随意行驶的汽车，到处生长的大树(虽然棕榈树通常看起来像巨大的棍子)，要么是水下的桥梁，要么是河上的大块黑色-还有很多沉没的船。
当这个系统运行良好时，它绝对是令人惊叹的。巴塞罗那、柏林、旧金山、西雅图、纽约和其他使用微软摄影测量方法绘制的城市看起来都很棒-包括可能特别是在晚上。
我的i7-9700K配有NVIDIA 2070超级显卡，其渲染引擎从不让帧速率降至每秒30帧以下(对于飞行模拟器来说这完全没问题)，通常徘徊在40帧以上，所有的图形设置都调到了最大值，分辨率为2K。
然而，当事情不起作用时，其影响是明显的，因为它是如此明显。一些城市，如拉斯维加斯，看起来就像遭受了某种灾难，好像城市被遗弃了，大自然接管了(老实说，在拉斯维加斯大道的情况下，这听起来并不是一件坏事)。
谢天谢地，所有这些都是微软和ASOBO可以解决的。他们只需要调整他们的算法，而且因为很多数据都是流式传输的，所以更新实际上应该是自动的。事实上，他们还没有这样做，这有点令人惊讶。
当你得到飞行模拟器的那一天，你很可能会想要飞越你的房子。如果你住在合适的城市(以及该城市的合适部分)，你很可能会很幸运，能真正看到你的房子有其独特的质感。但对于包括伦敦在内的一些城市来说，这款游戏只显示标准纹理，虽然微软在匹配不做摄影测量的城市的建筑物轮廓方面做得很好，但奇怪的是，伦敦或阿姆斯特丹没有在名单上(尽管伦敦现在市中心显然有几台风力涡轮机)，而德国的明斯特则在名单上。
一旦你到达海拔高度，所有这些问题显然都会消失(或者至少你不会看到它们)。但考虑到这些图形，你会想要在2000英尺或更低的地方花很多时间。
在目前的状态下玩这款游戏，真正让我印象深刻的是，这些图形上的不一致是如何为剩下的体验设定标准的。该团队说，他们100%的重点是让模拟器尽可能逼真，但例如，虚拟空中交通管制往往不使用标准措辞，或者当你离开主要机场时，无法将你送到正确的起飞管制。飞机模型看起来很棒，感觉非常接近真实(至少对我自己驾驶的那些飞机来说是这样)，但有些模型目前显示的空速是错误的，例如。一些飞机使用现代玻璃驾驶舱与Garmin 1000和G3X，但这些仍然感觉到严重的限制。
但让我在这里说清楚。尽管如此，即使处于测试状态，飞行模拟器也是一个技术奇迹，随着时间的推移，它只会变得更好。
让我们简单介绍一下用户体验。在PC上安装(Xbox版将在未来的某个时候推出)是一个下载90 GB的过程，这样你也可以离线玩游戏。安装过程会询问您是否也可以处理流数据，这会很快累积起来。在重新安装游戏并进行了几次屏幕截图的飞行之后，游戏已经下载了大约10 GB--它的下载速度很快，如果你正在使用计量连接，你应该注意这一点。
经过长时间的安装后，您将看到一个菜单屏幕，允许您开始新的航班，参加着陆挑战之一或团队设置的其他活动(他们真的为自己的Courchevel风景感到自豪)，并通过游戏的飞行训练计划。
该培训部分将引导您完成八项活动，帮助您掌握驾驶塞斯纳152的基础知识。大多数只需要不到10分钟的时间，之后你会得到一些简报，但我不确定这是否足以让新手不会很快感到沮丧(而更高级的玩家无论如何都会干脆跳过这一部分)。
我大部分时间都在模拟客机上驾驶小型通用飞机，但如果你更喜欢波音747或空客320neo，你也可以选择，还有一些涡轮螺旋桨飞机和商务机。在正式发布之前，我会花更多的时间和他们在一起。所有的飞机内外细节都很精美，除了几个小错误，一切都按预期进行。
要真正开始玩游戏，您需要前往世界地图，并选择您想要从哪里开始飞行。这里最棒的是，你可以在地图上选择任何地点，而不仅仅是机场。例如，这使得开始飞越一座城市变得容易。当你放大地图时，你可以看到机场和地标(地标要么是德国的新天鹅堡(Neuschwanstein Castle)等真实景点，要么是有摄影测量数据的城市)。如果一个城镇没有摄影测量数据，它就不会出现在地图上。
到目前为止，航班计划功能还很基本。对于视觉飞行，您可以直接或VOR到VOR，仅此而已。对于IFR航班，您可以选择低空或高空航空公司。你不能真的调整其中的任何一个，只能接受模拟器给你的结果。这并不是真正的航班计划工作方式(至少你会想要考虑当地的天气)，所以如果你能更多地定制你的航线就好了。微软与NavBlue合作提供空域数据，尽管内置地图不会对这些数据做太多工作，甚至不会向你显示你所在空域的垂直边界。
总是很难比较飞机模型和它们对真实事物的反应。据我所知，至少我熟悉的单引擎塞斯纳在现实中的操控方式与我预期的大致相同。默认情况下，方向舵控制感觉有点过于敏感，但这相对容易调整。我只玩了HOTAS风格的操纵杆和方向舵设置。我不推荐玩鼠标和键盘，但你的里程数可能会有所不同。
实时交通运行良好，但我当地机场周围的通用航空交通似乎都没有出现，尽管微软的合作伙伴FlightAware显示了这一点。
至于一般的真实/人工智能流量，SIM在管理这方面做得相当好。在测试版中，你不会真的看到任何真正的航空公司的肝脏-至少在大部分情况下-我在最新的版本中偶尔看到联合航空的飞机。考虑到微软自己的一些视频，更多的视频很快就会出现。除了可以在SIM中飞行的内置模型外，飞行模拟器仍然缺少用于人工智能交通的其他飞机模型库，尽管我再一次假设这也在进行中。
我们离发射还有三周时间。我希望团队能够解决其中的许多问题，我们将重新检查所有这些问题以进行最终审查。我对这款游戏的现状感到失望的是，它经常是如此接近完美，以至于当它达不到这一点时，它就会特别刺耳，因为它会把你从体验中拉出来。
不过，不要误会我的意思，在FS2020飞行已经是一次很棒的经历。即使没有摄影测量，一旦你超过3000英尺左右，城市和乡村也会看起来很棒。天气和云模拟-实时的-击败了今天的飞行模拟器的任何附加功能。机场仍然需要工作，但有汽车在周围行驶，在飞机周围摇摇晃晃地走来走去，这有助于让世界感觉更有活力。风会影响湖泊和海洋上的波浪(以及机场上的风袜)。这是真正的下一代飞行模拟器。
微软和ASOBO必须在让飞行模拟器成为铁杆粉丝想要的SIM卡和引入新玩家的易用游戏之间保持微妙的平衡。我玩过90年代以来的每个版本的飞行模拟器，所以入门用的时间正好是零。我的感觉是，尽管微软在SIM卡中加入了登陆挑战和其他更具游戏性的元素，但仅仅是为了寻找美好时光的新玩家一开始可能会感到有点迷失。在新闻发布会上，Asobo团队经常强调，他们的目标是现实主义，而不是其他任何东西-我完全同意这一点。我们将拭目以待，看看这对休闲玩家来说是否也是一种有趣的体验。

But the new Flight Simulator is also still very much a work in progress, too, even just a few weeks before the scheduled launch date on August 18. It’s officially still in beta, so there’s still time to fix at least some of the issues I list below. Because Microsoft and Asobo Studios, which was responsible for the development of the simulator, are using Microsoft’s AI tech in Azure to automatically generate much of the scenery based on Microsoft’s Bing Maps data, you’ll find a lot of weirdness in the world. There are taxiway lights in the middle of runways, giant hangars and crew buses at small private fields, cars randomly driving across airports, giant trees growing everywhere (while palms often look like giant sticks), bridges that are either under water or big blocks of black over a river — and there are a lot of sunken boats, too.
 When the system works well, it’s absolutely amazing. Cities like Barcelona, Berlin, San Francisco, Seattle, New York and others that are rendered using Microsoft’s photogrammetry method look great — including and maybe especially at night.
   The rendering engine on my i7-9700K with an Nvidia 2070 Super graphics card never let the frame rate drop under 30 frames per second (which is perfectly fine for a flight simulator) and usually hovered well over 40, all with the graphics setting pushed up to the maximum and with a 2K resolution.
 When things don’t work, though, the effect is stark because it’s so obvious. Some cities, like Las Vegas, look like they suffered some kind of catastrophe, as if the city was abandoned and nature took over (which in the case of the Vegas Strip doesn’t sound like such a bad thing, to be honest).
  Thankfully, all of this is something that Microsoft and Asobo can fix. They’ll just need to adjust their algorithms, and because a lot of the data is streamed, the updates should be virtually automatic. The fact that they haven’t done so yet is a bit of a surprise.
  Chances are you’ll want to fly over your house the day you get Flight Simulator. If you live in the right city (and the right part of that city), you’ll likely be lucky and actually see your house with its individual texture. But for some cities, including London, for example, the game only shows standard textures, and while Microsoft does a good job at matching the outlines of buildings in cities where it doesn’t do photogrammetry, it’s odd that London or Amsterdam aren’t on that list (though London apparently features a couple of wind turbines in the city center now), while Münster, Germany is.
 Once you get to altitude, all of those problems obviously go away (or at least you won’t see them). But given the graphics, you’ll want to spend a lot of time at 2,000 feet or below.
  What really struck me in playing the game in its current state is how those graphical inconsistencies set the standard for the rest of the experience. The team says its focus is 100% on making the simulator as realistic as possible, but then the virtual air traffic control often doesn’t use standard phraseology, for example, or fails to hand you off to the right departure control when you leave a major airport, for example. The airplane models look great and feel pretty close to real (at least for the ones I’ve flown myself), but some currently show the wrong airspeed, for example. Some planes use modern glass cockpits with the Garmin 1000 and G3X, but those still feel severely limited.
 But let me be clear here. Despite all of this, even in its beta state, Flight Simulator is a technical marvel and it will only get better over time.
  Let’s walk through the user experience a bit. The install on PC (the Xbox version will come at some point in the future) is a process that downloads a good 90GB so that you can play offline as well. The install process asks you if you are OK with streaming data, too, and that can quickly add up. After reinstalling the game and doing a few flights for screenshots, the game had downloaded about 10GB already — it adds up quickly and is something you should be aware of if you’re on a metered connection.
  Once past the long install, you’ll be greeted by a menu screen that lets you start a new flight, go for one of the landing challenges or other activities the team has set up (they are really proud of their Courchevel scenery) and go through the games’ flight training program.
  That training section walks you through eight activities that will help you get the basics of flying a Cessna 152. Most take fewer than 10 minutes and you’ll get a bit of a de-brief after, but I’m not sure it’s enough to keep a novice from getting frustrated quickly (while more advanced players will just skip this section altogether anyway).
 I mostly spent my time flying the small general aviation planes in the sim, but if you prefer a Boeing 747 or Airbus 320neo, you get that option, too, as well as some turboprops and business jets. I’ll spend some more time with those before the official launch. All of the planes are beautifully detailed inside and out and except for a few bugs, everything works as expected.
 To actually start playing, you’ll head for the world map and choose where you want to start your flight. What’s nice here is that you can pick any spot on your map, not just airports. That makes it easy to start flying over a city, for example. As you zoom into the map, you can see airports and landmarks (where the landmarks are either real sights like Germany’s Neuschwanstein Castle or cities that have photogrammetry data). If a town doesn’t have photogrammetry data, it will not appear on the map.
 As of now, the flight planning features are pretty basic. For visual flights, you can go direct or VOR to VOR, and that’s it. For IFR flights, you choose low or high-altitude airways. You can’t really adjust any of these, just accept what the simulator gives you. That’s not really how flight planning works (at the very least you would want to take the local weather into account), so it would be nice if you could customize your route a bit more. Microsoft partnered with NavBlue for airspace data, though the built-in maps don’t do much with this data and don’t even show you the vertical boundaries of the airspace you are in.
  It’s always hard to compare the plane models and how they react to the real thing. Best I can tell, at least the single-engine Cessnas that I’m familiar with mostly handle in the same way I would expect them to in reality. Rudder controls feel a bit overly sensitive by default, but that’s relatively easy to adjust. I only played with a HOTAS-style joystick and rudder setup. I wouldn’t recommend playing with a mouse and keyboard, but your mileage may vary.
 Live traffic works well, but none of the general aviation traffic around my local airports seems to show up, even though Microsoft partner FlightAware shows it.
 As for the real/AI traffic in general, the sim does a pretty good job managing that. In the beta, you won’t really see the liveries of any real airlines yet — at least for the most part — I spotted the occasional United plane in the latest builds. Given some of Microsoft’s own videos, more are coming soon. Except for the built-in models you can fly in the sim, Flight Simulator is still missing a library of other airplane models for AI traffic, though again, I would assume that’s in the works, too.
  We’re three weeks out from launch. I would expect the team to be able to fix many of these issues and we’ll revisit all of them for our final review. My frustration with the current state of the game is that it’s so often so close to perfect that when it falls short of that, it’s especially jarring because it yanks you out of the experience.
 Don’t get me wrong, though, flying in FS2020 is already a great experience. Even when there’s no photogrammetry, cities and villages look great once you get over 3,000 feet or so. The weather and cloud simulation — in real time — beats any add-on for today’s flight simulators. Airports still need work, but having cars drive around and flaggers walking around planes that are pushing back help make the world feel more alive. Wind affects the waves on lakes and oceans (and windsocks on airports). This is truly a next-generation flight simulator.
  Microsoft and Asobo have to walk a fine line between making Flight Simulator the sim that hardcore fans want and an accessible game that brings in new players. I’ve played every version of Flight Simulator since the 90s, so getting started took exactly zero time. My sense is that new players simply looking for a good time may feel a bit lost at first, despite Microsoft adding landing challenges and other more gamified elements to the sim. In a press briefing, the Asobo team regularly stressed that it aimed for realism over anything else — and I’m perfectly ok with that. We’ll have to see if that translates to being a fun experience for casual players, too.
title: 微软新的飞行模拟器是一件正在进行中的美丽作品
title_en: Microsoft’s new Flight Simulator is a beautiful work in progress

它是UFO爱好者梦寐以求的东西--或者是阴谋论者的噩梦。
美国军方关于“不明空中现象”的未发表调查结果可能很快就会公之于众。
但事实证明，当谈到飞碟等的透明度时，美国可以从加拿大学到一些东西。
上周，“纽约时报”(New York Times)援引参议院委员会的一份报告称，五角大楼已积极调查有关不明飞行物的报道，该报告称该项目为“不明空中现象特别工作组”。
根据这份报告，如果国会通过情报授权法案，五角大楼可能会被命令总结并公布该项目的调查结果。
克里斯·鲁特科夫斯基(Chris Rutkowski)承认，对于UFO的“狂热”粉丝来说，这是一个令人兴奋的时刻。克里斯·鲁特科夫斯基(Chris Rutkowski)是一名爱好者，也是关于这种现象多年来在加拿大发生的10本书的作者。
住在温尼伯的鲁特科夫斯基说：“加拿大实际上走在了这条曲线的前面。”
去年，他将个人收藏的UFO报告捐赠给了马尼托巴大学，其中包括1万份加拿大政府文件和2万多份来自不同机构的文件。他说，他通过获取信息的请求获得了一些文件，但其他文件则是他只是从加拿大交通部的报告中收集的，可以在网上获得。
加拿大政府拥有一个可供公众搜索的政府档案，其中包括可追溯到20世纪50年代的不明飞行物的政府记录。
大约9000份政府文件-从国防部关于“飞碟”的备忘录到加拿大皇家骑警在全国各地调查不明飞行物目击事件的官员的报告-都可以在加拿大图书馆和档案馆(Library And Archives Canada)的网站上找到。
Rutkowski说，这些文件“显示了几乎到目前为止，加拿大官方对这一问题的参与和兴趣的相当一致的记录”。“加拿大人遥遥领先于美国，因为我们有大量证据和文件显示官方对不明飞行物感兴趣。”
Rutkowski说，加拿大当局历史上比他们的美国同行更透明，加拿大人更多地将UFO视为一种科学好奇，而不是军事威胁。
他说，虽然加拿大政府可能还没有公布它所知道的有关不明飞行物的所有信息，但有时也有很好的理由对信息进行分类，从军事原因到个人隐私问题。在他看来，这不一定是“阴险”掩盖的迹象。
“他们没有隐瞒有关‘绿色小家伙’的信息，”他在谈到加拿大政府时说。
Rutkowski说，从20世纪50年代到1995年，国家研究委员会研究陨石，经常依靠UFO的报告来寻找坠落的陨石样本。他解释说，追踪天空中射出灯光的报告有时意味着调查UFO。Rutkowski说，加拿大皇家骑警通过调查UFO报告并将其发送给科学家来协助研究委员会。
国防部表示，目击不明空中物体的情况将报告给加拿大民用航空服务机构NAV Canada。
“就我们而言，我们只关注可信的威胁，这超出了我们的行动范围，”DND在一份电子邮件声明中说。
加拿大交通部汇编来自NAV加拿大以及其他来源的航空报告，并将其输入一个名为民航每日事件报告系统的可公开搜索的数据库。
加拿大交通部的一位发言人指出，虽然UFO是外星人的同义词，但在一定程度上要归功于好莱坞，但UFO这个词可以指很多东西-包括“看到无人驾驶飞行器(UAV或无人机)、气球、流星、天气现象和鸟类”，并补充说，UFO“不应该被解释为来自外星的东西。”
在加拿大交通部的民航每日事件报告系统中搜索“不明飞行物”，会得到14个结果。
其中一个条目描述了不列颠哥伦比亚省鲁珀特王子附近的一个“橄榄球大小和形状”的飞行物体，另一个条目引用了渥太华附近一个类似银色气球的物体，它有“机械凹槽”和“下面四个闪烁的灯”。在某些情况下，北美防空司令部(NORAD)接到了通知。
Rutkowsky从加拿大各地收集UFO的报告，并对他的发现进行年度总结。2019年，提交了849份UFO报告，遵循了过去十年UFO报告数量下降的模式。
Rutkowski说，在美国，对UFO的研究往往呈现出更具军国主义色彩的基调。
用你需要知道的一切开始你的早晨，没有什么是你不知道的。注册First Up，“星报”最新的每日电子邮件时事通讯。
现在就报名。
Rutkowski说：“在冷战期间，人们担心人们看到的是俄罗斯喷气式飞机的入侵。”这些军事文件中有许多是保密的，这引发了关于政府掩盖与不明飞行物相关的发现等持续不断的阴谋论。
Rutkowski说，最近关于美国参议院委员会报告的新故事，以及可能公布的先前关于不明飞行物的机密信息，使关于UFO的讨论重新成为主流。
在多伦多大学教授天文学的迈克尔·里德(Michael Reid)说，大多数天文学家对美国最近的发展持保留态度。
他说，根据他的经验，有关不明飞行物的报道“几乎总是两件事中的一件--要么是行星，要么是卫星。”
里德说，事实上，德克萨斯大学的天文学家通常只花不到百分之一的时间来研究不明飞行物。但该大学有时确实会收到他们无法解释的不明飞行物报告。
“偶尔我们会接到一个电话，我们会说，‘是的，我们不知道是什么。要么是你的描述不足以让我们弄清楚，要么是你看到了我们看不懂的东西。‘“。
这种不确定性对天文学家来说并不新鲜。里德说，事实上，与未知事物打交道是他们工作的核心部分。
“那里确实有一些我们不知道的东西。如果你是天文学家，那是你全部的事。这就是我们的纪律：努力弄清楚宇宙中正在发生的事情。“。
“那里有数十亿颗类似地球的行星。看起来那里很有可能存在智慧生命。“他说。“但这是一个非常不同的问题--那些生命会造访地球吗？”
事实上，世界各地的科学家都在操作测量望远镜，不断地拍摄天空的照片，并能够探测到另一个星系即将到来的彗星、小行星或恒星爆炸。
“这近乎令人怀疑，(这些望远镜)永远不会发现任何东西，而美国军方却可以。我们已经用一种非常科学的方式实践了对未知的研究。“。
更正-2020年7月29日：本文已被编辑，更正了加拿大图书馆和档案馆的名称。
note_en:
It’s the stuff of UFO enthusiasts’ dreams — or conspiracy theorists’ nightmares.
 Unpublished U.S. military findings about “unidentified aerial phenomena” could soon be made public.
 But America, it turns out, could learn a thing or two from Canada when it comes to transparency about flying saucers and the like.
 Last week, the New York Times reported that the Pentagon has actively investigated reports of UFOs, citing a Senate committee report that called the program the “Unidentified Aerial Phenomenon Task Force.”
 The Pentagon could be ordered to summarize and release the program’s findings if Congress passes the Intelligence Authorization Act, according to the report.
 It makes for an exciting time for “ardent” fans of UFOs, acknowledges Chris Rutkowski, an enthusiast and the author of 10 books about how the phenomenon has played out in Canada over the years.
 “Canada is actually ahead of the curve,” said Rutkowski, who lives in Winnipeg.
 Last year, he donated his personal collection of UFO reports, including 10,000 Canadian government documents and 20,000 more from various agencies, to the University of Manitoba. He said he obtained some of the documents through access-to-information requests but others he has simply collected from Transport Canada reports, available online.
   The Canadian government hosts a  publicly searchable archive of government records about UFOs dating back to the 1950s.
 About 9,000 government documents — ranging from defence department memos about “flying saucers” to RCMP reports by officers who investigated UFO sightings across the country — are available on the Library and Archives Canada website.
 The documents “show a fairly consistent track record of Canadian official involvement and interest in the subject, almost to the present time,” Rutkowski said. “People in Canada have been way ahead of the United States in the sense that we’ve had a body of evidence and a body of documentation showing official interest in UFOs.”
 Rutkowski says Canadian authorities have historically been more transparent than their U.S. counterparts and that Canadians saw UFOs more as a scientific curiosity rather than a military threat.
 While the Canadian government probably hasn’t released everything it knows about UFOs, he said, there are sometimes good reasons to classify information, from military reasons to individual privacy concerns. It isn’t necessarily a sign of an “insidious” coverup, in his view.
 “They’re not hiding information about ‘the little green guy,’ ” he said of the Canadian government.
 From the 1950s to 1995, the National Research Council studied meteorites and often relied on reports of UFOs to find samples of fallen meteorites, said Rutkowski. Tracking reports of shooting lights in the sky sometimes meant investigating UFOs, he explained. The RCMP assisted the research council by investigating UFO reports and sending them to the scientists, said Rutkowski.
 The Department of National Defence said sightings of unidentified airborne objects are reported to NAV Canada, the country’s civil air navigation service.
 “As far as we are concerned, we only focus on credible threats, and this falls outside the scope of our operations,” DND said in an emailed statement.
 Transport Canada compiles aviation reports from NAV Canada as well as other sources and enters them into a publicly searchable database called the  Civil Aviation Daily Occurrence Reporting System.
 Though synonymous with aliens, thanks in part to Hollywood, the term UFO can refer to many things, noted a spokesperson for Transport Canada — including “sightings of unmanned air vehicles (UAVs or drones), balloons, meteors, weather phenomena and birds,” adding that the term UFO “should not be interpreted to mean something of extraterrestrial origin.”
  A search for “unidentified flying object” in Transport Canada’s Civil Aviation Daily Occurrence Reporting System yields 14 results.
  One entry describes a flying object “the size and shape of a rugby ball” near Prince Rupert, B.C., and another cites a silver balloon-like object with “mechanical grooves” and “four blinking lights underneath” near Ottawa. In some cases, the North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD) was notified.
 Rutkowsky collects reports of UFOs from across Canada and produces an annual summary of his findings. In 2019, 849 UFO reports were filed, following a pattern of decline in the number of UFO reports in the past decade.
 In the United States, the study of UFOs often takes on a more militaristic tone, said Rutkowski.
 Start your morning with everything you need to know, and nothing you don't. Sign up for First Up, the Star's new daily email newsletter.
 Sign Up Now
 “During the Cold War, there was a concern that what was being seen by people was an incursion by Russian jets,” Rutkowski said. Many of those military files were classified, giving rise to persistent conspiracy theories about government coverup of UFO-related findings and more.
 Recent new stories about the U.S. Senate committee report and the possible release of previously classified information about unidentified flying objects has brought discussion about UFOs back into the mainstream, Rutkowski said.
 Most astronomers are taking recent developments in the U.S. with a grain of salt, said Michael Reid, who teaches astronomy at the University of Toronto.
 He said in his experience, reports of UFOs are “almost always one of two things — it’s either a planet, or it’s a satellite.”
 In fact, astronomers at U of T generally spend less than one per cent of their time investigating UFOs, Reid said. But the university does sometimes receive reports of UFOs they can’t explain.
  “Once in a while we get a call and we say, ‘Yeah, we don’t know what it is. Either your description wasn’t enough for us to figure out, or maybe you saw something that we can’t understand.’”
 That type of uncertainty is not new for astronomers. In fact, dealing with the unknown is a central part of their job, Reid said.
 “There are genuinely things up there that we don’t know what they are. If you’re an astronomer, that’s your whole business. That’s our discipline: trying to figure out what is going on in the cosmos.”
  “There are billions and billions of Earth-like planets out there. It seems pretty possible that there could be intelligent life out there,” he said. “But it’s a really different question to ask — does that life visit Earth?”
  In fact, scientists around the world operate survey telescopes that constantly take photographs of the sky and are able to detect an incoming comet, an asteroid or a star explosion in another galaxy.
 “It borders on incredulity that (these telescopes) would never spot anything whereas the American military does. We already practise the study of the unknown in a very scientific way.”
  Correction — July 29, 2020: This article has been edited to correct the name of Library and Archives Canada.
title: 不明飞行物的秘密文件？在加拿大，真相就在那里-在线和可搜索
title_en: Secret UFO files? In Canada the truth is out there – online and searchable

note:
我们最喜欢的轶事之一说明了让人们阅读这些文档有多难，它来自一个意想不到的来源：范·海伦(Van Halen)。
这支摇滚乐队因在演唱会上对后台设置提出异想天开和奇怪的具体要求而臭名昭著。乐队的标准合同长达53页，包括从“酸奶油鲱鱼”到“一大管肯德基果冻”的各种要求。但在“麦奇饼”部分，我们发现了臭名昭著的“M&Amp；Ms条款”--警告：绝对不能买棕色的。
因此，每个地点都会有一位幸运的人被派去把棕色的M&Amp；Ms从黄色、绿色和橙色的同行中拿出来。请记住，这是在20世纪80年代，所以当时实际上有两种深浅不一的棕色M&amp；M&M；M&amp；Ms，标准棕色和棕褐色，最终被淘汰，取而代之的是蓝色。
如果在后台发现任何棕色M&amp；Ms，乐队可能会取消整场演唱会，费用由赞助商承担全部费用。
事实，就像它经常发生的那样，要务实得多。乐队预料到没有人会阅读合同，于是设计了一个简单的测试，以确保他们所有的技术规范都被彻底阅读并遵守到T级。
范海伦的主唱大卫·李·罗斯(David Lee Roth)说：“范海伦是第一个在全国范围内使用850盏标准杆灯--巨型灯光的人。”“当时，这是有史以来最大的一部作品。”如果没有具体的指导方针，地板可能会弯曲倒塌，横梁可能会断裂，乐队、他们的工作人员和歌迷的生命可能会处于严重危险之中。
因此，如果棕色M&Amp；Ms在后台的糖果碗里，范海伦猜测，表演中更重要的方面-灯光、舞台、安全、票务-可能也是由粗心大意的推广者搞砸的。
“我来到后台。我找到了一些棕色的M&Amp；Ms，我走进了莎士比亚式的“这是我面前是什么！”……。你知道，一只手拿着头骨...。并迅速捣毁了更衣室。扔掉自助餐，在门上踢了个洞，价值一万两千美元的乐趣。
舞台在他们的地板上下沉。他们没有费心去看重量要求或其他任何东西，这导致他们的新地板下沉，对竞技场的地板造成了价值8万美元的损失。整个东西都得换掉。据媒体报道，我发现了棕色的M&Amp；Ms，并对后台区域造成了价值8.5万美元的破坏。
无论是合同、应用程序的服务条款、设计文档，还是您公司内部的维基，文档都很少能从目标受众那里得到彻底的通读。敏捷文档的TAGRI原则--“他们是不会读的”--不仅适用于软件开发，而且适用于所有类型的内部文档。
如果没有人会阅读您的设计文档或维基条目，为什么要浪费时间编写和维护它们呢？这经常会导致恶性循环：作家们觉得不值得花时间来正确地记录东西，因为反正没人会去读它，而且读者从来不会费心去阅读文档，因为它总是不完整或质量不高。
没有文献，所有的知识都是通过口头传授的。在一定程度上，当你的团队位于同一地点，而且规模很小时，它可能会奏效-也就是说，除非有人被公交车撞了。
但一旦事情开始扩大，问题就会出现。新员工加入，改变项目，调动团队，生病，度假，离开公司。他们随身携带着他们的知识-知识有时是在多年学习贵公司技术堆栈、内部流程和诀窍的错综复杂的过程中积累起来的。其结果是，新员工培训花费的时间更长，错误不断出现，生产率没有提高。
是的，人们不看文档。但这并不意味着它们根本就不应该写--相反，它们应该是为不读书的人设计的。
大多数人不会把你的文档从头到尾看完，不管它有多完美。不是因为他们懒惰，而是因为他们太忙了。正确阅读文档可能需要几个小时，而且大多数人没有那么多空闲时间。
事实是，最好的文档并不是用来阅读的。事实上，试图编写文档并期望其被读取往往是首先注定要失败的。
人们实际做的是查阅文档，这通常发生在另一个活动的上下文中。事实是，他们参考它，这就是创建它和做好它的充分理由。
您的团队与内部文档交互的方式对他们的工作效率有很大影响：
每当他们有问题并停下来在你的维基里查找一些东西时，他们的工作效率就会暂停。
如果他们找不到需要的东西，他们要么放弃这项任务，要么不得不发明一个新的方法来解决它，导致即使他们确实重返工作岗位，他们的生产率也会大幅下降。
文档的目标是使您的团队能够在他们需要的时候尽可能短的时间内找到他们需要的信息。这是为了帮助他们工作，然后不妨碍他们。这很少意味着要从头到尾阅读任何东西。因此，与其试图约束您的团队或欺骗他们阅读所有内容，我们需要训练自己设计、编写和组织我们的文档，以便有效地进行咨询。
当您使用单一来源的信息时，您会努力将其记录在一个地方，并且只记录在一个地方。不要指望人们愿意浪费时间在过时或重复的融合页面、随机的Google Drive文件夹或陈旧的电子邮件中寻找。使他们能够轻松快捷地查找信息--只有当所有答案都集中在一个地方时，才能实现这一点。
与流行的关于敏捷文档的误解相反，它并不是不存在的。它只是得到了有效的处理，在实践中，这确实证明了比传统主义者写的要少得多的文件。
不要仅仅为了拥有全面的文档而记录文档。写下你需要的东西，并且只写你需要的东西，心中要有明确的目的和受众。坚持一致的内容结构，指定一名“维基园丁”来修剪和编辑文档，保持维基的轻量级和可管理性。
如果没有人能找到伟大的内容，那么它就毫无用处。您的大多数读者不会滚动您的目录或您提供的任何其他导航工具-他们将使用搜索功能来查找他们要查找的内容。
无论您选择何种内部维基或文档工具来组织信息，快速可靠的搜索都是您需要的最重要的功能。当您有数千个文档时，您最不想要的就是手动滚动浏览它们，或者等待2分钟的搜索才能得到结果。您的内容还需要设计为可被发现，这意味着包括相关的关键字变体以匹配常见查询。
你越容易让人们找到他们需要的东西，他们就越有可能定期咨询医生。
我们没有耐心，注意力持续时间比金鱼短。为了被适当地吸收，信息需要以一种适应这一点的方式组织起来。
避免涉及广泛主题的长而多页的文档，保持文档简短而具体。提供足够的上下文，并使用内部链接来引导想要了解更多内容的读者。
使用“Brown M&amp；M”测试，并将隐藏的清单隐藏在你的文档中，可以帮助你当场抓住懒惰的读者，但如果你写文档的方式适应了不愿阅读的人性，那么你的努力将会有更好的回报。
note_en:
One of our favorite anecdotes that illustrates just how hard it is to get people to read the docs comes from an unexpected source: Van Halen.
 The rock band was notorious for the whimsical and oddly specific demands about their backstage set-ups at concerts. Spanning a whopping 53 pages, the band’s  standard contract included demands ranging from “herring in sour cream” to a “large tube of KY jelly.” But it’s in the “Munchies” section where we find the infamous “M&Ms clause”–  WARNING: ABSOLUTELY NO BROWN ONES.
  So, a lucky someone at each venue was given the task of plucking the brown M&Ms away from their yellow, green, and orange counterparts. Keep in mind, this was in the 1980s, so there were actually  two shades of brown M&Ms at the time, the standard brown and tan, which was eventually  eliminated to make way for blue.
 If any brown M&Ms were found backstage, the band could cancel the entire concert at the full expense of the promoter.
   The truth, as it often is, is much more pragmatic. Anticipating that no one's going to read the contract, the band devised a simple test to ensure that all their technical specifications had been thoroughly read and followed to a T.
 “Van Halen was the first to take 850 par lamp lights – huge lights – around the country,” said David Lee Roth, the lead singer of Van Halen. “At the time, it was the biggest production ever.” Without specific guidelines, floors could buckle and collapse, beams could rupture, and the lives of the band, their crew, and fans could be at serious risk.
 So, if brown M&Ms were in the backstage candy bowl, Van Halen surmised that more important aspects of a performance – lighting, staging, security, ticketing – may have also been botched by an inattentive promoter.
 “I came backstage. I found some brown M&Ms, I went into full Shakespearean “ What is this before me!?” ... you know, with the skull in one hand ... and promptly trashed the dressing room. Dumped the buffet, kicked a hole in the door, twelve thousand dollars’ worth of fun.
 The staging  sank through their floor. They didn’t bother to look at the weight requirements or anything, and this sank through their new flooring and did eighty thousand dollars’ worth of damage to the arena floor. The whole thing had to be replaced. It came out in the press that I discovered brown M&Ms and did eighty-five thousand dollars’ worth of damage to the backstage area.
  Whether it's a contract, an app's terms of service, a  design document, or your internal  company wiki, docs rarely get a thorough read-through from their target audience. The  TAGRI principle of  agile documentation –“ They Ain’t Gonna Read It” – doesn't just apply to software development but to all kinds of internal documentation.
  If no one's going to read your design doc or wiki entry, why waste time writing and maintaining them?  All too often, this leads to a vicious circle: writers don't feel it's worth their time to properly document stuff since no one's going to read it anyway, and readers never bother reading the documentation since it's always incomplete or of low quality.
   Without documentation, all knowledge is passed down via an oral tradition. It may work, to a point, when your team is co-located, and small – that is,  unless someone gets hit by a bus.
 But as soon as things start to scale, problems arise. New people come on board, change projects, move teams, get sick, go on vacation, leave the company. They  take their knowledge with them – knowledge sometimes accrued over years of learning the intricacies of your company’s technology stack, internal processes, and know-how. As a result, new employee training takes longer, mistakes are repeated, productivity doesn't ramp up.
 Yes, people don't read the docs. But that doesn't mean they shouldn't be written at all – rather, they should be designed for people who don't read.
  Most people aren't going to read your documentation cover to cover, no matter how perfect it is. Not because they are lazy –  they are too busy getting work done. Properly reading the docs can take hours, and most don't have that much time to spare.
 And the truth of the matter is, the best documentation is not designed to be read. In fact, trying to write documentation with the expectation of it being read is often what dooms it in the first place.
 What people actually do is  consult documentation, which usually takes place in the context of another activity. And the fact that they consult it is more than sufficient justification for creating it, and for doing it well.
  The way your team interacts with your internal docs has a big impact on their productivity:
 Every time they have a question and pause to look something up in your wiki,  there is a pause in their productivity.
  If they fail to find what they need, they either give up on the task or have to invent a new way to solve it, resulting in a  significant slowdown in their productivity even after they do get back to work.
 The goal of documentation is to enable your team to find the information they need when they need it, in as little time as possible. It's meant to aid them in their work and then stay out of their way. That rarely means reading anything cover to cover. So rather than trying to discipline your team or trick them into reading everything, we need to train ourselves to design, write, and organize our docs to be effectively consulted.
  When you  single-source information you strive to record it in one place and one place only. Don't expect people to be willing to waste time hunting through outdated or duplicated Confluence pages, random Google Drive folders, or stale emails. Enable them to look things up quickly and effortlessly – which can only be achieved if all answers are in one place.
   Contrary to popular misconceptions about agile documentation, it's not nonexistent. It's merely handled effectively, which, in practice, indeed proves to be a lot less documentation than what traditionalists write.
 Don't document just for the sake of having comprehensive documentation. Write down what you need and only what you need, with a clear purpose and audience in mind. Stick to an agreed-upon content structure and appoint a “ wiki gardener” to prune and edit down the docs, keeping the wiki light and manageable.
  Great content is useless if no one can find it. Most of your readers aren't going to scroll through your table of contents or any other navigation tools you provide – they will use the search function to find what they are looking for.
 Whatever  internal wiki or documentation tool you choose to organize information, fast and reliable  search is the single most important feature you will need. When you have thousands of documents, the last thing you want is to manually scroll through them or wait for a 2-minute search to yield the results. Your content also needs to be designed to be discovered, which means including relevant keyword variations to match the common queries.
 The easier you make it for people to find what they need, the more likely they would be to consult the docs regularly.
  We are impatient and have a  shorter attention span than a goldfish. To be properly absorbed, information needs to be organized in a way that accommodates that.
 Avoid long multi-page documents that cover broad topics, keep your docs short and specific. Provide just enough context, and  use internal links to guide the readers who want to learn more.
   Using the “Brown M&M” test and burying hidden checklists in your docs could help you catch the lazy readers red-handed, but your efforts will have a much better pay-off if the way you write your docs accommodates the human nature of not wanting to read them.
title: 布朗M&M，或为什么没人读手册？
title_en: Brown M&Ms, or Why No One Reads the Manual
